title: Spring Breakout

jokes.text: Michael calls Lucille the laziest person in the world from across the room, while she sits doing nothing amid the mess
jokes.setup: Michael complains about the mess not being cleaned up after fumigation
jokes.punchline: He threatens to slap her face but notes she's 'all the way on the other side of the room' — both acknowledging his own laziness in the accusation and the absurd smallness of the distance
jokes.explanation: Classic Lucille characterization — her laziness is so extreme even a threat of violence is half-hearted. The line works because it's perfectly in character and the self-undermining logic is tight.

jokes.text: Narrator reveals Michael asked Lucille to call the fumigators 'long before he began hiding his fugitive father in the attic'
jokes.setup: Michael scolds Lucille for not calling the fumigators, which she did do
jokes.punchline: Narrator reveals the fumigation was arranged before Michael stashed his fugitive father in the attic — making the whole scolding self-incriminating and absurd
jokes.explanation: The narrator drops the bomb about the fugitive father hiding in the attic with perfect economy and complete deadpan. The irony is layered: Michael is scolding someone while doing something far worse. Strong Arrested Development narrator joke — information delivery that itself is the comedy.

jokes.text: George Sr. yells 'Polly, cover your mouth!' to a parrot as the fumigators are called
jokes.setup: Michael is notified in time about the fumigation — narrator says 'luckily'
jokes.punchline: George Sr. is hiding in the attic with a parrot named Polly, whom he frantically tries to protect from fumigation fumes
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of a fugitive in an attic worrying about his parrot's gas mask situation is a strong visual. Sets up future Polly callback. Characteristic AD layered absurdism.

jokes.text: Narrator reveals Lucille didn't actually clean the house — she intercepted Lupe (her mother's housekeeper) mid-commute to do it for her
jokes.setup: Michael says Lucille has only cleaned the house once under their deal
jokes.punchline: She didn't even do it that once — she intercepted Lupe arriving for work at Lucille's mother's house and had her do it instead
jokes.explanation: This is quintessential Arrested Development — the reveal that Lucille's one instance of 'doing the right thing' was actually a more elaborate form of cheating. The callback visual of Lupe in the car mid-commute, the narrator's dry delivery, and Lucille's perfect heel characterization all combine. High craft.

jokes.text: 'Almost done. Yours or mister's?' (Lupe doing the Bluths' laundry in the car)
jokes.setup: Lucille offers Lupe a ride 'the rest of the way' — implying neighborly kindness
jokes.punchline: Lupe is folding laundry in the car, asking whose underwear she's working on — the 'ride' is cover for exploiting Lupe's labor
jokes.explanation: The comedic image of Lupe folding laundry in the passenger seat while Lucille drives is perfectly economical storytelling. 'Yours or mister's?' lands because it's so matter-of-fact. Strong visual gag.

jokes.text: [Narrator] 'Lupe never accepted a ride from Lindsay again.'
jokes.setup: The laundry-in-the-car revelation about Lucille exploiting Lupe
jokes.punchline: Narrator tags the scene by noting Lupe never accepted a ride from Lindsay (not Lucille) — implying Lindsay did something even worse off-screen
jokes.explanation: This is a masterclass in AD narrator comedy. The whole scene is about Lucille, then the narrator name-drops Lindsay in the button — implying a separate, undescribed atrocity committed by Lindsay that was so bad Lupe swore off rides entirely. Maximum funny per word, completely earned, and the imagination fills in something worse than what was shown. One of the episode's best jokes.

jokes.text: Michael didn't notice Tobias had moved out; he only noticed they'd stopped TiVo-ing The Christopher Lowell Show
jokes.setup: Lindsay asks if Michael noticed Tobias had moved out
jokes.punchline: Michael noticed only because The Christopher Lowell Show (a gay interior design show) stopped being TiVo'd — implying he paid closer attention to TV habits than to his brother-in-law
jokes.explanation: Layered joke: Michael's obliviousness, Tobias's ongoing closeted-gay subtext (Christopher Lowell being a flamboyant gay decorator), and the mundane TV detail as the tell. Rewards rewatching and familiarity with Tobias's arc.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Well, excuse me for liking the way they shape my junk.' / Lindsay: 'You can pack your junk and get out.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay catches Tobias wearing her underwear; he defends himself about how they 'shape' things
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's furious 'pack your junk' double-entendre — meaning both his belongings and his genitalia, simultaneously ending the marriage
jokes.explanation: The double meaning of 'junk' is perfectly constructed and perfectly timed. Tobias wearing Lindsay's underwear is already a strong character joke, but 'pack your junk' as the episode-triggering line is tight, quotable, and completely character-specific. High craft.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'You're kicking me out? At the beginning of pilot season?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay kicks Tobias out for wearing her underwear
jokes.punchline: Tobias's objection is entirely about pilot season — his delusions about his acting career override his concern about the marriage ending
jokes.explanation: Pure character comedy — only Tobias would respond to being kicked out of a failing marriage by objecting to the timing relative to TV casting cycles. The line is instantly quotable and perfectly characterizes his delusional actor identity.

jokes.text: Tobias takes up residence in the apartment of 'Detective Frank Wrench' on the set of the TV show 'Wrench'
jokes.setup: With nowhere to go after Lindsay kicks him out, Tobias needs lodging
jokes.punchline: He moves into a TV set apartment — a fake apartment on an active production set — treating it as real housing
jokes.explanation: The concept of Tobias living on a TV set — with all the absurdist implications (non-functional toilets, actors arriving, etc.) — is original and pays off repeatedly in the episode. Strong setup for later gags.

jokes.text: Michael: How's Maeby taking it? / Lindsay: 'She was so excited the hot water was lasting longer. How could I have the heart to tell her it's because her father moved out?'
jokes.setup: Michael asks how Maeby is handling Tobias leaving
jokes.punchline: Lindsay reveals Maeby doesn't know — she's only noticed the hot water improvement caused by Tobias's absence, which Lindsay finds too poignant to correct
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed joke with genuine emotional texture. The hot water detail is specific, funny, and sad — Tobias's absence is an improvement by every domestic metric, yet Lindsay frames it with mock-maternal tenderness. Dark, earned, original.

jokes.text: Tobias moved out? / 'Did he take the good video camera with him?'
jokes.setup: Michael tells George Michael that Tobias moved out
jokes.punchline: George Michael's only concern is whether Tobias took the camera — revealing complete indifference to the family upheaval in favor of his magic video project
jokes.explanation: Solid character beat — the whole family's self-absorption illustrated in a single line. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Gob's magic video project: 'I'm calling it Tricks- Let me finish- around the Office.'
jokes.setup: Gob explains he's making a video of himself doing magic tricks around the office
jokes.punchline: He anticipates the obvious '...around the Office' double meaning and preemptively cuts off anyone from finishing — which draws more attention to it
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The structure is brilliant: Gob is aware the name sounds sexual, interrupts himself to preempt the joke, and in doing so makes the joke land harder. 'Let me finish' inserted in the middle is perfectly placed. Only Gob could deliver this with such wounded dignity. Quotable, tight, character-perfect.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I figured out a way to make money while I'm working.' 'That is what we call working.'
jokes.setup: Gob explains his side-hustle magic video as 'making money while working'
jokes.punchline: Michael flatly points out that what Gob describes is simply working — the joke is Gob's complete inability to understand basic employment
jokes.explanation: Clean, economical character joke. Michael's exasperation lands well, but the concept isn't especially novel for Gob.

jokes.text: 'Is that Qusay Hussein at the omelet bar?'
jokes.setup: The family watches what they think is a home video (their 40th anniversary party)
jokes.punchline: George Sr. casually identifies Qusay Hussein in the party footage — revealing the Bluth family had business dealings with Iraqi dictators
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical dark joke that simultaneously advances character backstory, rewards plot-aware viewers, and lands as a standalone absurdist gag. The specificity of 'at the omelet bar' makes it exceptional.

jokes.text: 'Look at this. If these guys move, they don't get paid.' (reaction to Iraqi workers being filmed)
jokes.setup: George Sr. watches the party video where Iraqi workers appear to be on site
jokes.punchline: He's proud that his illegal labor practices are captured on tape, commenting on the workers' compliance with casual pride
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s casual self-incrimination is on-brand. Works as character comedy but is slightly overshadowed by the Qusay joke that precedes it.

jokes.text: Narrator reveals George Michael re-enacted Star Wars scenes — including 'You cut off my hand!' / Gob: 'Worth every penny.'
jokes.setup: The family watches what they think is an anniversary party home video
jokes.punchline: It's actually George Michael's childhood Star Wars re-enactment, in which he had someone cut off his hand — Gob declares it 'worth every penny' of the $2.1M company money spent
jokes.explanation: Outstanding escalating reveal. The joke builds through multiple layers: (1) it's not the party tape, (2) George Michael was a Star Wars obsessive, (3) he actually re-enacted it seriously at age 13, (4) there was apparently a real budget, (5) George Sr. is outraged about the money, (6) Gob loved it anyway. 'Worth every penny' is a perfect button. Rewatch value is high — foreshadows Buster's hand loss.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Not so. Buster had once videotaped himself re-creating moments from his then-favorite film, Chicago.' / [Buster trilling and dancing] / 'He also liked Star Wars.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. calls George Michael's Star Wars tape 'the lamest thing ever put on tape'
jokes.punchline: Narrator corrects him — Buster has a tape of himself doing Chicago dance numbers, and also Star Wars. The 'He also liked Star Wars' button is delivered with complete narrator deadpan.
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's finest moments. The structure is perfect: challenge ('lamest thing ever'), correction (Buster's Chicago tape — already surreal), physical comedy of Buster trilling and dancing, and then the devastating, completely deadpan afterthought: 'He also liked Star Wars.' The economy of that final tag line — doing so much with so few words — is exceptional. Pure Arrested Development.

jokes.text: Michael talks up George Michael's self-esteem: 'He's such a great kid, and everybody loves him. He's George Michael!'
jokes.setup: Michael worries about George Michael's self-esteem
jokes.punchline: Michael's pep talk references 'George Michael' — the famous singer — as if his son's name itself is a source of social cachet, without apparently realizing this might be a liability rather than an asset
jokes.explanation: Running gag about the character's name double as the pop star. Works within the show's universe but is functional rather than exceptional.

jokes.text: 'Unless he knocks her up like you did with his mother. Plus, she's religious. If that one gets pregnant, it stays pregnant.'
jokes.setup: Michael is musing that George Michael will move on from Ann
jokes.punchline: Michael goes dark — invoking accidental pregnancy, then pivoting to Ann's religion meaning she'd never abort, revealed as his actual concern
jokes.explanation: Punchy dark comedy. The escalation from 'knocks her up' to the abortion joke is quick and the punchline lands. The self-referential guilt about George Michael's own conception adds a layer.

jokes.text: 'Believe me. I dated a chick like that once in high school. No, I didn't.'
jokes.setup: Michael is speaking as though from experience about religious girls
jokes.punchline: He immediately self-corrects mid-sentence, revealing it was an invented claim — his self-awareness punctures his own bravado
jokes.explanation: Tightly structured self-correction joke. Michael's goody-two-shoes character correcting himself mid-boast is perfectly in character. The economy is excellent — three words ('No, I didn't.') fully land the punchline. Feels very true and specific.

jokes.text: Michael interrupts Ann and George Michael's intimate-sounding exchange about 'doing it' — it's about cigarettes
jokes.setup: Michael hears Ann: 'I want you to do it.' / George Michael: 'I'm gonna lose it before I start.'
jokes.punchline: Michael panics ('Don't start!'), but they're talking about smoking — specifically invoking Sammy Davis Jr. as a cautionary tale
jokes.explanation: Solid misdirection — the sexual innuendo setup resolves to a smoking conversation. The Sammy Davis Jr. non-sequitur adds an extra comedic beat. Functional misdirection that's well-executed.

jokes.text: 'Smoking. It's a killer. That Sammy Davis Jr. should still be performing.'
jokes.setup: Ann is advising George Michael not to start smoking
jokes.punchline: Her specific invocation of Sammy Davis Jr. (who died of throat cancer) as a cautionary tale is delivered with total sincerity — the bathos of the reference is the joke
jokes.explanation: Ann's earnest moralizing paired with the oddly specific Sammy Davis Jr. reference is a good character comedy beat. The deadpan sincerity is funny.

jokes.text: Star Wars theme plays when Michael suggests George Michael run for president because 'you are George Michael!'
jokes.setup: Michael pumps up his son's name as if it's inherently cool
jokes.punchline: The Star Wars theme plays — referencing both the earlier tape and the name's association with the musician/franchise
jokes.explanation: Visual/audio gag that works as a callback to the earlier tape reveal. Functional running gag but not a standout joke on its own.

jokes.text: Michael's convoluted non-answer about whether he won the student body president election: 'Didn't say I won. Said I can't remember... I seem to remember the other guy won the title, although there were plenty who wanted to overturn the decision... I said let's not put the school through that.'
jokes.setup: Ann asks if Michael was student body president — he initially implies he won
jokes.punchline: His increasingly elaborate non-denial denial about losing mirrors political scandal rhetoric — he lost by 2%, apparently, and chose not to contest it, framing it as nobility
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of political spin — Michael's inability to simply say 'I lost' escalates into full Al Gore territory. The self-righteous nobility of 'I said let's not put the school through that' is perfectly absurd. Strong character comedy with excellent structural escalation.

jokes.text: Ann's rhapsodizing about the first time with George Michael: 'It's not just gonna be my love and George Michael's, but God's love as well. God, it's gonna be incredible.'
jokes.setup: Ann talks about waiting for the physical relationship and how special it will be
jokes.punchline: She invokes God's love being 'in' the act, then uses 'God' as an exclamation of excitement — creating an unintentional (for her) blasphemous double meaning
jokes.explanation: Ann's oblivious blasphemy is perfectly crafted — she says 'God, it's gonna be incredible' immediately after saying God will be in their sexual encounter, in complete sincerity. Her character's religious naivety is the engine. Strong joke with cringe and dark comedy elements.

jokes.text: George Michael says 'I'll do it. I'm pumped.' — Michael thinks he means the campaign, Ann thinks she meant... something else
jokes.setup: Ann's God/sex speech — Michael tries to redirect to the campaign
jokes.punchline: 'Campaign?' — George Michael had meant the campaign all along, but the scene has been loaded with sexual double meanings throughout
jokes.explanation: The scene's final misdirection beat — functional but the joke has been running for a while and this is the weakest version of it. Still lands due to accumulated context.

jokes.text: Buster complaining about his hook arm: 'I can't put the aorta in with this thing.' / Lucille: 'Oh, stop complaining.' / Buster: 'I'm sorry I'm an inconvenience to you, Mother.'
jokes.setup: Buster, who lost his hand to a seal, is now dealing with the hook prosthetic
jokes.punchline: Lucille's total indifference to her son's disability struggles — his having no hand is simply an inconvenience she refuses to sympathize with
jokes.explanation: Lucille's callousness toward Buster's disability is on-brand. The specificity of the medical puzzle context (aorta) is a nice touch. Character comedy works well here.

jokes.text: Lucille screams in horror at Buster's prosthetic/stump situation; Buster whimpers
jokes.setup: Buster is struggling with puzzle
jokes.punchline: Something about Buster's hand situation causes Lucille to recoil in theatrical horror
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy beat that lands on Lucille's horror rather than sympathy. Good character work but relatively broad. The whimper is a Buster specialty.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'As Lupe felt sorry for Buster's loss of a hand, Lindsay felt sorry for her loss of Lupe.'
jokes.setup: Buster is upset about losing his hand
jokes.punchline: Narrator draws an equivalency between Buster losing a hand and Lindsay losing a housekeeper — treating them as parallel tragedies
jokes.explanation: Classic AD narrator equivalency joke. The parallelism structure is textbook Arrested Development — treating Lindsay's petty problem with identical narrative gravity as a genuine tragedy. Economy is exceptional.

jokes.text: Lindsay discovered a garbage problem from cleaning one room — and found eBay while stashing trash
jokes.setup: Lindsay had cleaned one room and now has garbage to deal with
jokes.punchline: Instead of taking the garbage out properly, she finds eBay — presumably to sell things — which immediately becomes her new distraction from any further cleaning
jokes.explanation: 'I haven't seen this in ages. EBay.' — the delivery implies she treats eBay like an old friend she's been separated from. Good Lindsay character moment: she was one step away from completing a chore and got distracted by online shopping.

jokes.text: Lindsay, searching for somewhere to dump garbage, discovers eBay: 'I haven't seen this in ages.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay cleaned one room and now has a garbage problem
jokes.punchline: Instead of solving the garbage problem, she discovers eBay — suggesting she plans to sell rather than dispose of things, and the parenthetical 'it was a new friend to George Sr.' implies Bluth illegal activity
jokes.explanation: The eBay gag works on multiple levels: Lindsay's laziness/greed, and the cut to 'unfortunately it was a new friend to George Sr.' suggesting the fugitive attic-dweller is also on eBay. Good layering.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Unfortunately, it was a new friend to George Sr.' — implying George Sr. is also using eBay from the attic
jokes.setup: Lindsay discovers eBay happily
jokes.punchline: George Sr. (hiding in the attic) has also discovered eBay — a fugitive conducting commerce from an attic, framed as a regrettable coincidence
jokes.explanation: Good narrator economy — 'unfortunately' doing heavy lifting. The image of a fugitive on eBay in an attic is inherently funny. Works as narrative connector.

jokes.text: Gob arrives at the board meeting in a tuxedo: 'Well, maybe it's time for a little... Office Magic.'
jokes.setup: A formal board meeting is in progress
jokes.punchline: Gob interrupts with a formal magic show announcement in a board meeting context — the tonal mismatch is the joke
jokes.explanation: Gob's showmanship in a corporate setting is a reliable joke. The callback to the 'Office Magic' concept pays off but isn't exceptional standalone.

jokes.text: Gob's magic trick: 'Sometimes it's as simple as turning 10:45 in the morning into lunchtime!'
jokes.setup: Gob promises Office Magic
jokes.punchline: His first trick is simply... declaring it's lunchtime — the bathos of the 'magic' being a verbal announcement is the joke
jokes.explanation: The gap between Gob's theatrical presentation and the lameness of the 'trick' is well-executed. Classic Gob: maximum showmanship, minimum substance.

jokes.text: Gob pulls a chicken out of his magic act at the board meeting — 'Maybe you'd prefer some chicken instead!'
jokes.setup: Gob is doing board room magic
jokes.punchline: He produces an actual live chicken as a magic trick at a formal business meeting
jokes.explanation: Physical visual comedy that works in the moment. The chicken is a classic absurdist prop in a corporate context. Functional slapstick.

jokes.text: The board member Gob thought was an investor voted against Michael because he thought Gob was an actor/entertainment
jokes.setup: Michael needed Gob at the meeting to vote for the building renovation
jokes.punchline: Gob's magic show made the board member think Gob was hired entertainment, not a fellow investor, so he voted against — the exact opposite outcome Michael needed
jokes.explanation: The board member thinking Gob is hired entertainment rather than a president/stakeholder is an earned consequence of the Office Magic sequence. Good structural payoff — Gob's actions directly cause the opposite of what was needed.

jokes.text: Gob fires Michael; Michael fires Gob back; they argue over who fired whom while Gob can't figure out how to call Security
jokes.setup: Michael threatens to fire Gob for ruining the vote
jokes.punchline: They both claim to have fired each other, then Gob attempts to call Security while Michael has to explain 'pound is tic-tac-toe' — the absurdity of two brothers in a corporate power struggle over a phone extension code
jokes.explanation: 'Pound is tic-tac-toe, right?' — Gob's incompetence with a phone while trying to assert corporate authority is perfectly bathetic. The mutual firing is a great escalation beat that feels true to both characters.

jokes.text: Gob: 'No. I'm the president.' Michael: 'No. You lost the presidency, just like you did in high school when you refused to play dirty.' Gob: 'Worked when I ran.'
jokes.setup: Michael and Gob argue about who is president of the company
jokes.punchline: Gob references high school elections — revealing he won by playing dirty, while Michael (the ethical one) lost by 2%. The callback to the earlier election discussion lands with additional irony.
jokes.explanation: Good callback that adds new information: Gob won by playing dirty, Michael lost by being principled. This deepens the character contrast and sets up the campaign subplot. Well-structured.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'The guard had recently lost half his tie to Office Magic.'
jokes.setup: The security guard enthusiastically agrees to escort Michael out
jokes.punchline: Narrator explains his eagerness: Gob destroyed half his tie in a previous magic trick — the guard has personal motivation
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy — the narrator delivers the whole backstory in one clause. The image of a half-destroyed tie is funny and the callback to Gob's magic mishap is efficient. Strong AD narrator technique.

jokes.text: Camera operator to Gob: 'Cheat out. You have to cheat out a little bit.'
jokes.setup: Gob is filming his magic video with the company camera
jokes.punchline: The camera operator gives Gob direction as if he were an actor, breaking the fourth wall by referencing actual filmmaking technique — and 'cheat out' as directing advice is absurdly specific
jokes.explanation: Meta joke about filmmaking that works on two levels — it's specific industry language and it characterizes Gob as so desperate for an audience he's hired a crew. Clever.

jokes.text: Michael to Maeby: 'Your uncle doesn't not-work here anymore.'
jokes.setup: Michael needs to explain that Gob has been fired
jokes.punchline: The double negative 'doesn't not-work' is Michael's tortured way of saying Gob was fired — since Gob never worked there in any real sense, the negative is genuinely ambiguous
jokes.explanation: Genuinely clever wordplay. 'Doesn't not-work' perfectly captures Gob's never-having-really-worked status AND his firing simultaneously. The grammatical logic is airtight and funny. Quotable and economical.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'Maybe I should just start keeping some secrets of my own.' (to camera)
jokes.setup: Maeby is upset that everyone keeps secrets from her
jokes.punchline: The line foreshadows Maeby's own secret-keeping (her movie studio job) — played completely straight but lands as dramatic irony for viewers who know her secret
jokes.explanation: Characteristically layered AD moment — funny as dramatic irony, and sets up further Maeby storylines. Works better on rewatch.

jokes.text: 'Marry me.' / 'I'm so sorry. I forgot who I was talking to.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Maeby why she's not at school
jokes.punchline: Maeby reflexively says 'Marry me' — her go-to deflection for awkward questions — then Michael remembers he should know better than to ask and apologizes
jokes.explanation: The 'Marry me' running gag is perfectly executed here — and Michael's apology for forgetting is the sharper half. His self-correction acknowledges the gag has been earned and he should know better. Two-tiered payoff.

jokes.text: 'That's like comparing apples and some fruit nobody's ever heard of.'
jokes.setup: Michael says George Michael will win the election
jokes.punchline: Maeby dismisses the comparison between George Michael and Steve Holt — using an idiom that perfectly captures how unknown/uncool George Michael is relative to Steve
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best one-liners. 'Some fruit nobody's ever heard of' is a brilliantly constructed insult — it uses the apple idiom but subverts it in the most specific, dismissive way possible. Quotable, tight, and sharply observed about teenage social hierarchies.

jokes.text: 'He might even finish after the Indian kid.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is explaining how far down the pecking order George Michael is
jokes.punchline: The casual admission that George Michael might not even beat the third-place candidate (Rav Nadir) lands as both a burn and a setup for the episode's eventual punchline (Rav wins 97%)
jokes.explanation: A good setup joke that pays off enormously at the episode's end. On first viewing it's a mild additional burn; on rewatch it's brilliant foreshadowing.

jokes.text: Lucille fires Lupe — but Buster says he's firing her too, then Lucille clarifies: 'I was firing Lupe!' / Buster: 'Well, that makes more sense.'
jokes.setup: Lucille discovers Lupe — apparently in a compromising position (implied by Lucille's gasp and 'And yet you're too good to polish the candlesticks?')
jokes.punchline: Both mother and son try to fire each other simultaneously; Buster's 'Well, that makes more sense' acknowledges the absurdity of the crossed wires
jokes.explanation: Good comic confusion with strong character voices. Buster's matter-of-fact 'Well, that makes more sense' after realizing his mother was firing Lupe (not him) is a nice understated button.

jokes.text: Lucille discovers Buster being tended to by Lupe — 'And yet you're too good to polish the candlesticks?'
jokes.setup: Lucille walks in and gasps to find Lupe attending to Buster
jokes.punchline: Rather than concern about what Buster needs or why Lupe is there, Lucille's immediate response is jealousy that Lupe is serving Buster instead of polishing candlesticks for her
jokes.explanation: Lucille treating Buster's care needs as a personal affront to her household management is perfectly in character. 'Polishing the candlesticks' as her priority is a good specific detail.

jokes.text: Lupe: 'I no does Buster anymore.' / Lindsay: 'Fine. I'll DustBuster! God.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay asks Lupe to at least 'DustBuster' even after being fired. Lupe says she won't 'do Buster' anymore.
jokes.punchline: Lupe's English conflates 'DustBuster' and 'do Buster' (Buster the character) — implying she was sleeping with Buster, which is why Lucille fired her. Lindsay has to do the DustBusting herself.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional wordplay that retroactively explains the earlier scene (what was Lupe doing with Buster that warranted firing). 'I no does Buster anymore' is a brilliantly constructed line — Lupe's ESL syntax creates the double meaning perfectly. The implication (Lupe was sleeping with Buster) is delivered with complete economy and massive payoff.

jokes.text: Tobias calling from the set bathroom: 'I don't think this is a real toilet.' [tapping]
jokes.setup: Tobias is living in the Detective Wrench apartment on a TV set
jokes.punchline: He's calling from the set bathroom — which has a fake toilet — while the show films around him
jokes.explanation: The fake toilet detail is a great absurdist image. Works well as a comedic development of the 'living on a TV set' premise.

jokes.text: Man on set: '...dirty cop, so Frank Wrench is gonna clean ya.' / [sounds of a scuffle] / 'Why is this door locked?'
jokes.setup: Tobias is on the phone hiding in the bathroom while they're filming
jokes.punchline: The show dialogue ('Frank Wrench is gonna clean ya') bleeds into the real action of finding Tobias in the bathroom — dramatic cop dialogue meets mundane bathroom intrusion
jokes.explanation: Nice tonal contrast between the hard-boiled TV show dialogue and the absurd situation. Works as part of the Tobias-on-set premise.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'We're gonna use your idea and highlight being chaste.' Campaign poster reveals: 'GEORGE MICHAEL: TASTE THE CHASTE.'
jokes.setup: Michael suggested highlighting being chaste for the Christian demographic
jokes.punchline: The poster 'TASTE THE CHASTE' is a terrible pun that sounds like a suggestive ad campaign — exactly the wrong message for a chastity platform
jokes.explanation: The 'TASTE THE CHASTE' poster is a perfectly ironic slogan — meant to appeal to Christian voters but inadvertently sounding like a candy bar tagline with sexual undertones. Well-constructed visual gag.

jokes.text: Kids chanting 'Four more years! Four more years!' for George Michael's campaign announcement
jokes.setup: George Michael announces he's running for student body president for the first time
jokes.punchline: 'Four more years' is a political reelection chant — he's a first-time candidate, making the chant completely nonsensical and indicating the kids don't know what they're chanting
jokes.explanation: Absurdist political gag — using an incumbent chant for a first-time candidate. The crowd's enthusiasm is funny precisely because the chant makes no sense in context.

jokes.text: Kids chanting 'Four more years!' for George Michael's student body president campaign — an absurd presidential campaign chant for a high school election
jokes.setup: George Michael has gathered supporters for his student body president campaign
jokes.punchline: The students chant 'Four more years!' — a presidential re-election chant for someone who has never held office and is running for the first time in a high school
jokes.explanation: Gentle absurdism — the re-election chant for a first-time candidate is funny in its misapplication of political theater to a high school context. Not the sharpest joke but works as background texture.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Well, guess who's been fired.' Lucille: 'Michael fired you?' Gob: 'No. I fired him. As a result, I'm no longer welcome in the office.'
jokes.setup: Gob arrives at Lucille's claiming to have been fired
jokes.punchline: He corrects — he fired Michael, but consequently lost access to the office. The self-defeating logic is pure Gob.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Gob — he 'won' the firing battle and completely lost the war, and explains this with such bewilderment. The logical inversion is sharp and character-specific.

jokes.text: Lucille introduces a Roomba as her new housekeeper replacement: 'It's a robot. I hate it. I miss Lupe.'
jokes.setup: Lucille announces she's found a replacement for Lupe
jokes.punchline: It's a Roomba — she immediately hates it and misses Lupe, having replaced a person with a robot she can't emotionally connect with
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag — Lucille's instant regret and emotional attachment to Lupe contrasted with cold technology. The quick pivot to 'I miss Lupe' is a nice character beat.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I won't let you go down that road. I want you to be strong, and you don't need the comfort of an immigrant in Mother's old stirrup pants to make you feel that way.'
jokes.setup: Lucille says she misses Lupe
jokes.punchline: Gob's motivational speech ends with a very specific and odd image — Lupe wearing Lucille's stirrup pants — that speaks to both the family's dependency on Lupe and Gob's total obliviousness to how condescending he's being
jokes.explanation: Gob's unintentionally offensive pep talk is good character comedy. The stirrup pants detail is specifically absurd and the condescension is perfectly oblivious.

jokes.text: Roomba beeps — Gob: 'Oh. You're hungry.' / [Roomba powers down]
jokes.setup: Gob has just told Lucille to be strong and not need the Roomba
jokes.punchline: The Roomba beeps and Gob anthropomorphizes it (calling it 'hungry'), then it immediately powers down — undercutting the whole pep talk
jokes.explanation: Gob's immediate bonding with the Roomba after telling Lucille not to depend on it is good irony. The power-down timing is a nice physical comedy beat.

jokes.text: Tobias appears at Lindsay's door in full drag as 'Mrs. Phyllidia Featherbottom' — the housekeeper from 'the agency'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is desperate for a new housekeeper
jokes.punchline: Tobias has disguised himself as a female housekeeper — his drag performance is clearly unconvincing, but Lindsay doesn't recognize her own husband
jokes.explanation: The Mrs. Doubtfire premise is explicitly acknowledged by the narrator — the joke is that the show is so meta it names the film it's ripping off. Tobias in drag is a strong visual, and Lindsay not recognizing her husband is perfectly in character for a couple so estranged they've lost each other's faces.

jokes.text: Tobias/Phyllidia offers to 'sing a song or two, if it comes in handy' — then performs a creepy musical number about medications
jokes.setup: Tobias is performing the role of housekeeper Phyllidia Featherbottom
jokes.punchline: He breaks into a Mary Poppins-style song about frosting on your throat before medications — an inexplicably specific and dark variation
jokes.explanation: The Mary Poppins parody with Tobias/Phyllidia singing about medication prep is delightfully weird. 'A squirt of frosting on your throat before we take our medications' is both a parody of Spoonful of Sugar and oddly specific. The narrator explicitly noting 'there was also some Mary Poppins in there' is a funny meta-comment.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'It was the exact plot of the film Mrs. Doubtfire.'
jokes.setup: Tobias dressed as 'Mrs. Featherbottom' sings and cleans the house
jokes.punchline: The narrator flatly names the movie being plagiarized — treating the show's derivative premise as a neutral factual observation
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest moments. The narrator calling out the exact film being copied — with complete deadpan — is masterful meta-comedy. It preempts the audience's criticism by making the show smarter than the criticism. Economy is near-perfect: six words do everything. Exceptional AD narrator voice.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'There was also some Mary Poppins in there.'
jokes.setup: Already told us it was Mrs. Doubtfire
jokes.punchline: Tacks on a second cinematic reference — Mary Poppins — as an afterthought, implying Tobias's performance is a compound plagiarism
jokes.explanation: The callback to the Mrs. Doubtfire line, with an additional film, is funny because it escalates the self-awareness. The throwaway addition of Mary Poppins works as a topper.

jokes.text: Michael arrives and compliments the clean house — Lindsay takes credit, and Michael notes: 'I think he misses his daughter.' revealing he recognized Tobias instantly
jokes.setup: Mrs. Featherbottom has cleaned the house; Lindsay accepts credit
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately knows it's Tobias — the disguise that fools Lindsay (who lives with him) doesn't fool her brother-in-law. Michael's 'I think he misses his daughter' is the understated reveal.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Michael's complete lack of surprise — his deadpan 'I think he misses his daughter' says volumes about how transparent Tobias's disguise is, and how far Lindsay and Tobias have drifted. Good character economy.

jokes.text: 'I think he misses his daughter.' — Michael noticing Tobias/Featherbottom's behavior, without recognizing it's Tobias
jokes.setup: Mrs. Featherbottom is acting strangely (apparently staring at Maeby with emotion)
jokes.punchline: Michael accurately diagnoses the situation — the nanny misses her daughter — without realizing the nanny IS the father
jokes.explanation: Classic dramatic irony — Michael is completely right and completely oblivious. The layering of correct observation/wrong context is a reliable AD engine. 'That's convenient, considering you didn't even have the courage to tell Maeby he moved out' as Lindsay's retort adds another layer of irony.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I'm not that perfect. I pushed George Michael into running for office, and I think he's gonna get crushed by this kid Steve Holt.' Lindsay: 'Yeah. I'd vote for Steve.'
jokes.setup: Michael and Lindsay argue about parenting
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's instant declaration that she'd vote for Steve Holt over her own nephew is perfectly selfish — she's clearly voting on attractiveness
jokes.explanation: Quick and character-perfect. Lindsay's total self-absorption — voting for the hot kid over her own nephew — is funny in its honesty.

jokes.text: George Sr.'s religious epiphany in the attic, invoking 'Our Heavenly Father let his son die so our sins could be absolved' — Michael: 'Great. So you're a Christian now.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. has had a divine intervention in the attic
jokes.punchline: Michael's flat, skeptical 'Great. So you're a Christian now' treats the epiphany as an inconvenient plot development rather than a spiritual event
jokes.explanation: Michael's exasperated deadpan is well-deployed. The joke is that George Sr.'s spiritual awakening is treated as yet another inconvenience by his long-suffering son.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'You and Gob were like those biblical brothers, Gallant and, um... Goofuth.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is referencing the Bible to explain why Michael should find Gob
jokes.punchline: His 'biblical brothers' are actually Gallant and Goofus — from Highlights magazine's 'Goofus and Gallant' — which George Sr. clearly doesn't realize isn't the Bible. 'Goofuth' is his mangled attempt.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding joke that requires cultural knowledge (Goofus and Gallant from Highlights) and works on multiple levels: George Sr.'s new-found religiosity mixed with his shaky biblical knowledge, the perfect aptness of the Goofus/Gallant metaphor for Michael/Gob, and 'Goofuth' as his sincere attempt at a biblical name. The narrator playing it completely straight ('Michael realized he had lived like Gallant') makes it even better.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Michael realized he had lived like Gallant, and if he wanted to help his son's election, he would have to find... Goofuth.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. invents 'Goofuth' as a biblical name
jokes.punchline: The narrator accepts 'Goofuth' as a real name and uses it — treating the nonsense word with full narrative gravity
jokes.explanation: The narrator's complete commitment to calling Gob 'Goofuth' — complete with dramatic pause and music sting — is the perfect button to the joke. The narrator's omniscient deadpan treating absurdist dialogue as canonical fact is peak AD.

jokes.text: Michael tells Gob: 'The guy he's running against is basically a young you.' Gob: 'Wow. It won't be easy to win a race against myself. Of course, if anyone can do it, I can.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Gob to help George Michael campaign against Steve Holt
jokes.punchline: Gob takes the 'young you' framing literally — turning it into a self-competition, then boasting he's the only one who could beat himself
jokes.explanation: This reveals that Steve Holt is Gob's son (implied by his age and physical resemblance), though neither knows it yet. The joke works perfectly on its surface — Gob's narcissism and logical incoherence — but rewards rewatch enormously when the reveal comes. 'Of course, if anyone can do it, I can' is perfectly quotable.

jokes.text: Michael: 'He doesn't need to beat him. He just needs to beat the Indian kid.'
jokes.setup: Gob thinks he needs to mount a major campaign against Steve Holt
jokes.punchline: Michael lowers the bar to 'just beat Rav Nadir' — setting up the eventual punchline that Rav wins 97% of the vote
jokes.explanation: Setup for the episode's final joke (Rav wins 97%). Functional comedy on first view; retroactively funnier when Rav obliterates the field.

jokes.text: Lucille says 'Robot'll get it' and the Roomba has knocked Lucille's (Buster's mother's) glass over — [Gasps, Robot Powers Down] Buster: 'What do you expect, Mother? I'm half machine! I'm a monster!'
jokes.setup: Lucille relies on the Roomba to clean
jokes.punchline: Buster identifies with the malfunctioning Roomba — declaring himself 'half machine' because of his hook hand, then collapsing into 'I'm a monster!' The Roomba's failure mirrors his own identity crisis.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's funniest character moments. Buster's melodramatic identification with a broken Roomba — 'What do you expect, Mother? I'm half machine!' — is perfectly absurd and character-specific. The hook hand/machine connection is brilliantly silly, and 'I'm a monster!' as a topper is genuinely delightful. High impact.

jokes.text: Gob's campaign strategy: 'Oh, we should definitely sacrifice the whole virgin thing. And I got the perfect way. We hand out rubbers that say, 'We salute George Michael.''
jokes.setup: Michael asks Gob for campaign strategy for George Michael
jokes.punchline: Gob's first move is to hand out condoms with the candidate's name — the most counterproductive strategy possible for a chastity-based campaign
jokes.explanation: Pure Gob — oblivious to the irony of handing condoms for a chastity campaign. 'We salute George Michael' is a brilliantly awful slogan for a condom. The campaign manager incompetence is perfectly pitched.

jokes.text: Gob to Michael: 'Good point, two percent.' Michael: 'Two percent, huh? Is that how little I lost by?'
jokes.setup: Michael says they shouldn't play dirty
jokes.punchline: Gob uses Michael's 2% election loss as a contemptuous nickname — and Michael's response reveals he's been dwelling on it
jokes.explanation: Sharp callback — 'two percent' as a contemptuous nickname is a great character beat for Gob. Michael's wounded response reveals how much the loss still stings decades later. Good economy.

jokes.text: 'She'll forgive ya. You're George Michael.' — Gob dismissing Ann's moral objection
jokes.setup: Michael says Ann is upset about the dirty tricks strategy
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'She'll forgive ya. You're George Michael.' — treating the singer's name as an inherent social status that compels forgiveness
jokes.explanation: Callback to Michael's earlier 'He's George Michael!' speech, now weaponized as romantic advice by Gob. The gap between the confidence of the claim and the reality of George Michael's actual social standing is the core joke.

jokes.text: Tobias (as Mrs. Featherbottom) singing to Maeby off-key, presenting her mother's banned fen-phen as 'Fun-fun': 'Oh! Well. You should always read the label'
jokes.setup: Mrs. Featherbottom (Tobias) is playing nanny to Maeby, giving her 'medicine'
jokes.punchline: The 'Fun-fun' is revealed to be fen-phen — an FDA-banned diet drug — which Tobias then sings about reading labels, completely oblivious
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy — a man in drag unknowingly giving a child banned diet pills while singing about label safety. The Mary Poppins parody tone makes it more absurd. Strong dark subversive element.

jokes.text: Tobias/Phyllidia takes a pill from Maeby — Maeby explains it's her mom's fen-phen, 'banned by the FDA'
jokes.setup: Tobias is playing nanny and trying to medicate the children
jokes.punchline: The 'medicine' offered is Lindsay's FDA-banned diet pills — Maeby helpfully identifying them as banned rather than hiding this fact
jokes.explanation: Maeby's matter-of-fact identification of the pills as FDA-banned is a sharp detail. The Bluths' cavalier relationship with dangerous pharmaceuticals is good dark character comedy.

jokes.text: Maeby clocks that 'Mrs. Featherbottom' calls her 'he' and says 'Mr. Fingerbottom?' — then is immediately shut down
jokes.setup: Mrs. Featherbottom says 'wherever your father is right now, she loves you very, very much' — a pronoun slip
jokes.punchline: Maeby responds 'Mr. Fingerbottom?' — catching the pronoun slip and the wrong name simultaneously, only to be immediately steamrolled
jokes.explanation: Maeby's sharp catch — wrong pronoun AND wrong name — is great character work for her. 'Fingerbottom' as her mishearing of 'Featherbottom' is funny in its own right. Tobias's immediate 'Mrs.' correction and pivot is a good beat.

jokes.text: Tobias as Mrs. Featherbottom: 'I'm sure wherever your father is right now, she loves you very, very much.'
jokes.setup: Tobias (disguised as a woman) is trying to connect with his daughter without revealing himself
jokes.punchline: 'She loves you very much' — referring to 'father' with a female pronoun, a Freudian slip that almost reveals his identity while also capturing Tobias's genuine gender confusion
jokes.explanation: This line is doing extraordinary work. 'She loves you very much' — referring to the father — is simultaneously: a near-reveal, a Freudian slip, a comment on Tobias's closeted identity, and earned character comedy. Only Tobias could deliver this line and mean it this many ways at once. High craft.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'Not enough to be honest with me.'
jokes.setup: Tobias/Featherbottom says her father loves her
jokes.punchline: Maeby's response — her father is literally in the room, in disguise — is delivered without any awareness of the irony, making it both emotionally true and comedically rich
jokes.explanation: Effective dramatic irony — her complaint that her father isn't honest with her is delivered directly to her disguised father. Earns emotional resonance while being funny.

jokes.text: Gob shouts 'Go home, you terrorist!' at the Indian student candidate's speech
jokes.setup: Rav Nadir gives a measured, sympathetic speech about his family's immigrant background
jokes.punchline: Gob's heckle is both outrageously offensive and strategically backwards — he's trying to help George Michael, but his heckling the only candidate beneath George Michael is self-defeating
jokes.explanation: Gob's racism and political incompetence collide. The joke is that this is his strategy — heckling the only guy they were supposed to beat. Dark but character-specific.

jokes.text: Rav Nadir's campaign speech: 'Drought, starvation — these are the things my family has had to deal with for generations, although, fortunately, not since we moved to Corona del Mar.'
jokes.setup: Rav Nadir is giving his campaign speech, beginning with a description of generational hardship
jokes.punchline: 'Although, fortunately, not since we moved to Corona del Mar' — instantly deflating the gravitas of immigrant suffering with a specific, wealthy Orange County suburb
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. The setup of drought and starvation as generational trauma is punctured by the clause 'not since we moved to Corona del Mar' — a specific, affluent Southern California suburb that does everything the punchline needs to do. The joke perfectly balances self-awareness (the character knows the contrast is funny) with observational accuracy about immigrant assimilation narratives. The specificity of 'Corona del Mar' rather than 'California' or 'America' is what elevates this to exceptional. Excellent structure, high originality, maximum economy.

jokes.text: 'How stupid do you have to be to say something like that? You're not wanted here!' — Michael to Gob, then Gob heckles Steve Holt: 'Sure you didn't make that video in a cave?'
jokes.setup: Michael scolds Gob for the terrorist comment; crowd agrees
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately pivots to heckling Steve Holt with a cave reference — post-9/11 bin Laden joke — showing he learned nothing from the previous heckle
jokes.explanation: The escalation of Gob's heckling — immediately doing it again after being called out — is good character comedy. The cave joke is another culturally offensive heckle, establishing Gob's complete tone-deafness.

jokes.text: Steve Holt's video is produced by Ann Veal. Gob shouts 'Evil!' at Ann on screen. Michael: 'This kid's popular?' Gob: 'Looks like a chick I did once in high school.' / 'Get a haircut!'
jokes.setup: Steve Holt's slick campaign video plays to massive crowd approval
jokes.punchline: Gob's running commentary — calling Ann evil, finding Steve familiar, and heckling his haircut — inadvertently sets up the Steve-is-Gob's-son reveal while demonstrating Gob's complete inability to stop heckling
jokes.explanation: Rich, multi-layered moment. Gob's 'Looks like a chick I did once in high school' is the seed of the Steve Holt parentage reveal — brilliant foreshadowing disguised as an insult. 'Evil!' at Ann is a running gag callback. Works on multiple levels simultaneously.

jokes.text: Steve Holt's video: 'Recently, I've been dealing with the fact that I've never known my father... but I finally found a new Father.' [Religious imagery, crowd goes wild]
jokes.setup: Steve Holt's campaign video appears to be about finding his biological father
jokes.punchline: He means God — 'a new Father' — the audience (in the gym) explodes with approval, while the audience (watching at home) processes the Gob connection
jokes.explanation: Effective misdirection — 'found a new Father' appears to be about God, but the show has seeded that Steve's real father is Gob. Works on first watch as a religious pivot and on rewatch as ironic foreshadowing.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I've made a huge mistake.' [They try to pull George Michael's video tape]
jokes.setup: After seeing Steve Holt's polished video, Michael realizes George Michael's tape is a disaster
jokes.punchline: 'I've made a huge mistake' is the Arrested Development recurring line — delivered here in perfect context
jokes.explanation: Series-wide running gag deployed at an appropriate moment. The line is maximally quotable and gets a strong reaction from fans. The originality score is lower because it's a formula at this point, but the placement is earned.

jokes.text: George Michael's campaign video plays: Gob on PA saying 'Steve Holt is a bastard. He doesn't even know who his real father is.'
jokes.setup: Michael tries to pull the tape but it's too late
jokes.punchline: The tape plays Gob calling Steve a bastard for not knowing his father — Gob is, as the show implies, Steve's actual father. The insult is delivered by the person most responsible for Steve's situation.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comedic and structural peak. Gob — almost certainly Steve's biological father — is on a PA system calling Steve a bastard for not knowing his father. The irony is savage and perfectly earned through the episode's careful setup. This will pay off even more in subsequent episodes when the parentage is confirmed. One of the finest jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: George Michael's campaign video continues: 'George Michael Bluth is a cool guy. His dad is a powerful executive working for this man.' [photo of George Sr. — fugitive criminal] / 'The girls like him just fine. Young and old. It doesn't matter. In the dark.'
jokes.setup: Gob produced a campaign video for George Michael
jokes.punchline: Every selling point backfires: 'powerful executive' cuts to a wanted fugitive, 'the girls like him' is ambiguous and weird, 'in the dark' makes it sound predatory
jokes.explanation: Escalating campaign disaster — each successive selling point is worse than the last. 'In the dark' as a button is particularly inspired. Gob's complete inability to read the room is on full display. Strong escalation structure.

jokes.text: 'The girls like him just fine. Young and old. It doesn't matter.'
jokes.setup: George Michael's campaign video is attempting to establish his popularity with girls
jokes.punchline: 'Young and old. It doesn't matter.' — this attempt to establish cross-demographic appeal sounds like a very different kind of endorsement
jokes.explanation: The 'young and old, it doesn't matter' as a description of cross-generational girl appeal is inadvertently sinister — it sounds like an endorsement of something illegal. Gob's confident delivery of this as a positive attribute is perfect.

jokes.text: 'Until this part.' — [Star Wars theme, George Michael's childhood re-enactment tape plays to the school audience]
jokes.setup: George Michael is mortified by Gob's awful campaign video
jokes.punchline: The narrator says 'Until this part' — the Star Wars re-enactment tape from the episode's opening plays in front of the whole school
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode callback — the Star Wars tape planted in the opening pays off here. The narrator's 'Until this part' sets up a beat of expectation, and then the tape plays to the entire school. 'I couldn't find another tape' as the reveal is devastating and funny. Structural excellence — the whole episode has been building to this.

jokes.text: George Michael's Star Wars video plays at school assembly — his light saber re-enactment on the big screen in front of the whole school
jokes.setup: Gob says 'I couldn't find another tape' — the Star Wars home video from the beginning of the episode is now playing at the school assembly
jokes.punchline: The kids laugh hysterically; George Michael has never been more embarrassed
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed long-form callback. The Star Wars tape was introduced early, used as a character humiliation device within the family, and now returns as the climactic public humiliation of the campaign. The structure across the episode is excellent — setup, development, payoff. 'I couldn't find another tape' is Gob's perfect excuse. The narrator's 'George Michael had never been more embarrassed — Until this part' is a great button.

jokes.text: Kids laugh hysterically at George Michael's Star Wars tape; 'I like Steve's more.'
jokes.setup: George Michael's Star Wars tape plays to the school
jokes.punchline: The assembled students prefer Steve's religious video to George Michael's tape — the Christian video beats the nerd video
jokes.explanation: Functional reaction beat. The crowd's rejection is the expected outcome. The joke is more in the situation than this specific line.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Guess I underestimated the religious vote.'
jokes.setup: Steve Holt's religious video crushes George Michael's campaign
jokes.punchline: Michael's deadpan political analysis of a high school election — 'the religious vote' — as though conducting a post-mortem
jokes.explanation: Competent deadpan. The political post-mortem tone for a high school election is mildly amusing. Functions well in the scene.

jokes.text: 'I like Steve's more.' — a student's reaction to George Michael's Star Wars video vs. Steve's religious video
jokes.setup: The school has now seen both campaign videos
jokes.punchline: The understated, sincere preference for the 'Footprints' religious video over the Star Wars cringe-fest is the ultimate verdict on George Michael's campaign
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective. The deadpan delivery implies no cruelty — just an honest preference — which is somehow more damning. Works as a button on the entire campaign sequence.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Well, that leaves three percent. You did better than I did when I ran. I tied with Bart Simpson and 'School Sucks.''
jokes.setup: George Michael gets 3% of the vote
jokes.punchline: Michael reveals he tied with Bart Simpson (a write-in) and 'School Sucks' (another write-in) — further humiliating his own election loss while trying to comfort his son
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the episode's ongoing student body president thread. Michael's loss is worse than implied — he didn't lose to a real person, he tied with Bart Simpson (a TV character) and a slogan. The specificity of 'Bart Simpson and School Sucks' is inspired. Quotable, earned, and the funniest version of Michael's self-deprecation in the episode.

jokes.text: 'I tied with Bart Simpson and "School Sucks."'
jokes.setup: Michael establishes he got 2% in his high school election
jokes.punchline: He actually tied with write-in candidates: Bart Simpson and 'School Sucks' — meaning he received as many genuine votes as protest write-ins for a cartoon character
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's absolute best jokes. The specificity of 'Bart Simpson and School Sucks' as Michael's rivals is pitch-perfect — it confirms not only that he lost by a humiliating margin but that his vote total was equivalent to protest votes. The 'School Sucks' write-in as a political slogan someone earnestly voted for is a perfect detail. Highly quotable, excellent economy, earned through the episode-long buildup of the election thread.

jokes.text: Kid yells 'Hey, it's Star Dork!' at George Michael. Michael: 'Hey, he knows you now.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is humiliated after the Star Wars tape
jokes.punchline: Michael tries to spin the humiliating nickname 'Star Dork' as a positive — 'he knows you now' — as a silver lining to public mockery
jokes.explanation: Michael's desperate optimism is well-deployed. 'He knows you now' as a spin on 'Star Dork' is the kind of self-defeating pep talk only a well-meaning but oblivious parent delivers. Good character beat.

jokes.text: Ann to George Michael about his lightsaber moves: 'George Michael, was that really you doing those lightsaber moves? You were incredible. I've only gotten better. You're still doin' that?' / Ann: 'So, you wanna grab a curtain rod and go over to my place?'
jokes.setup: George Michael is mortified about the Star Wars tape
jokes.punchline: Ann is genuinely impressed by the lightsaber moves and invites him to her place to do them with a curtain rod — the most wholesome/bizarre resolution possible
jokes.explanation: Ann's sincere enthusiasm for the lightsaber moves — and her curtain rod invitation — is sweetly absurd. Her complete earnestness in a moment of maximum social humiliation is endearing and funny. Good character work.

jokes.text: Michael watches George Michael leave happily with Ann: 'Gotta do something about that kid's self-esteem.'
jokes.setup: George Michael has been publicly humiliated but is now happy with Ann
jokes.punchline: Michael, who caused the humiliation, still thinks George Michael needs help — missing that his son is actually fine. The parental anxiety is misdirected.
jokes.explanation: Good bookend callback to the episode's opening self-esteem concern. The irony that George Michael needed no help but Michael's 'help' almost destroyed him is the episode's thesis. Functional callback.

jokes.text: 'On the next Arrested Development' — Lucille finds a new housekeeper: it's Tobias again (as Mrs. Featherbottom), giving Lucille a ride in a car while doing laundry — 'Yoo-hoo! I'll take you the rest of the way!'
jokes.setup: Narrator announces 'On the next Arrested Development' — Lucille is looking for a new housekeeper
jokes.punchline: Mrs. Featherbottom (Tobias) intercepts Lucille on the street offering a ride — mirroring the episode-opening Lupe/Lucille car gag exactly
jokes.explanation: Beautiful structural callback — the 'on the next' tag mirrors the episode opening precisely: a housekeeper offering a ride and doing laundry in the car. Merging two ongoing plots (Tobias's disguise and Lucille's housekeeper search) with the episode's opening image is excellent construction.

jokes.text: [Lucille and Tobias/Featherbottom both crying in the car while Tobias sings]
jokes.setup: Mrs. Featherbottom is driving Lucille while singing
jokes.punchline: Both Tobias and Lucille are crying during the car ride — an absurdly emotional moment with a man in drag and a narcissistic mother, both weeping for unrelated reasons
jokes.explanation: The visual of two people crying in a car — one in full elderly woman drag — for completely different reasons is a strong absurdist image. Works well as a 'next episode' tease.

jokes.text: 'On the next AD' — Steve Holt tracks down his real father: 'a scientist, a doctor, a senator?' / 'Well, we got some bad news.' / 'Wow. Is that what's gonna happen to my hair?'
jokes.setup: Steve Holt wants to find his biological father — he imagines impressive possibilities
jokes.punchline: The 'bad news' is apparently Gob — and Steve's first concern is whether he'll go bald like Gob. The parentage reveal is delivered through the mundane horror of inherited baldness.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding 'on the next' joke. The parentage reveal — Steve Holt is Gob's son — is delivered through the single detail of Gob's baldness. 'Is that what's gonna happen to my hair?' is the perfect character-specific reaction: not the existential horror of who his father is, but the vanity of inherited hair loss. Earned, structurally perfect, and quotable.

tmdb_overview: Gob confronts the editor of the videos that showcased his magic mistakes. Michael tricks Lucille in to rehab, and then attempts to get her out after George Sr. is kidnapped.
insight: Spring break chaos hits 77 jokes in 29 minutes—Bluth family dysfunction peaks here.
